topic: Build - cbindgen for Exporting Rust to C

turns.content: I'm trying to export a function from my Rust crate using cbindgen, but the generated header keeps missing my doc comments even though I added them. It seems like just annotating pub extern C fns isn't enough for documentation propagation.

That's a common gotcha when moving from scripting to Rust FFI. The issue is that cbindgen needs explicit metadata in your config file, not just the source code attributes. You need to configure the documentation propagation settings in your cbindgen.toml so it knows which parts of the crate to include.
Okay, I've got my pub extern C function defined with #[doc = "..."], but the header still shows nothing. Should I be using a different configuration key?

You're looking at the right problem area. In cbindgen.toml, you need to set the documentation propagation rules explicitly. The config keys are things like doc-propagation and which crates or modules should have their docs included in the generated header.
I added those settings but it's not working. Is there a specific trait or attribute I need to annotate on the function itself?

The function needs to be marked as pub and extern C, which you already have. But for docs to propagate correctly, cbindgen also looks at whether your crate is properly configured in cargo.toml with the right build features enabled.
Wait, so I need to check my Cargo.toml too? And maybe add some feature flags?
Yes, exactly. You might be missing the right feature flag in your Cargo.toml that enables cbindgen's documentation parsing capabilities.
Can you remind me what specific feature I should enable?

You need to ensure the build script is running with the right context and that your crate exports everything cbindgen expects. Try checking if your C header generation pipeline is actually invoking cbindgen in CI, because sometimes local runs miss configuration details.

topic: Build - cbindgen for Exporting Rust to C

turns.content: I'm preparing our team for an interview on FFI and I keep hearing that we just need a simple config file. Is there really only one way to export Rust functions to C?

That's a common misconception but not entirely accurate. While cbindgen does use a configuration file, the tooling itself isn't magic—it relies on strict adherence to Rust ABI rules and specific attribute annotations on your functions.
Right, so I assume if I just put #[no_mangle] on every public function it will generate headers automatically without me touching anything else?

You're close but that's a wrong assumption. The #[no_mangle] attribute is necessary to prevent mangled names, yet cbindgen also requires the functions to be annotated with pub extern C so it can recognize them as FFI entry points.
So I need to manually add pub extern C to my lib.rs? That feels like a lot of boilerplate compared to what I'm expecting.

Exactly, and that's where the confusion often starts. The tool doesn't magically infer visibility or linkage from everything; it specifically looks for functions marked with pub extern C in your crate root or module files.
Okay, so I annotate my functions correctly but then what? Do I just run cargo build and get a header file?
Not quite. You need to configure cbindgen.toml to specify the output directory and ensure your Rust code compiles into shared libraries or static archives that cbindgen can read.
Wait, so if I have a function that returns an Option<Vec<String>> will cbindgen even try to export it?
No, that's a critical failure point. Complex return types like Option or Vec are not stable ABI and cbindgen simply ignores them unless you explicitly tell it via annotations.
What does that even mean? I thought the tool would handle all Rust types automatically?

It handles basic primitives like i32, f64, and pointers but complex heap allocations or smart pointers require special handling. You must annotate those functions with #[allow(dead_code)] if the signature isn't perfect for C interop.
And what about documentation? Does cbindgen actually pull doc comments into the headers?

Yes, that's one of its main strengths. If your pub extern C functions have proper #[doc] attributes or regular doc comments above them, cbindgen will propagate that documentation into the generated .h files.
So I can skip writing a separate manual header file and just let it do the work?
Yes, provided your Rust code is annotated correctly. However you should still validate the generated headers in CI to catch any drift from your source changes.
Got it, so I need a script that runs cbindgen and compares the output against my expected header templates?
Precisely. You can write a simple shell or CI pipeline step that invokes cbindgen on your target directory and diffs the output against your reference headers to ensure nothing broke.
One last thing, what if I have multiple crates? Does cbindgen handle them separately?
Yes, you configure separate sections in your cbindgen.toml for each crate path so it generates distinct headers per library.

topic: Build - cbindgen for Exporting Rust to C

turns.content: Hey, so I'm trying to export some Rust functions to C using cbindgen. My first thought is that it's similar to generating headers in C++, like when you have a class and generate a header file, but with Rust. Am I on the right track?

Yes, exactly! That’s a good mental model. cbindgen helps automate creating a C-compatible header from your Rust code — kind of like how you’d write a class in Rust and then generate a C++ header using tools like SWIG or doxygen-style comments.
Right, so I define my functions with `pub extern "C" fn`, but wait, am I doing it right? Is there anything special about that?

Absolutely! When you declare a function as `extern "C"` in Rust, it tells the compiler and linker to use C calling conventions. This is crucial for FFI (Foreign Function Interface) because it ensures ABI compatibility with C code. So yes, your approach is correct.
Okay, so now I have my functions exported. What about the cbindgen.toml file? Is that just like a Cargo.toml for configuration?

Yes! It’s similar in purpose but scoped specifically to exporting your Rust code into C headers. You put it at the root of your crate, and configure options like output path, language, or even exclude certain modules.
I see. And then I annotate my functions with comments, just like doxygen? That helps propagate documentation?

Absolutely! If you use `///` or `/** */` style comments above your exported functions, cbindgen will include those in the generated C header. This is very handy for maintaining API docs across languages.
Sweet. So I run cbindgen as part of my build? That’s easy enough?

Yes, you can add it to your `Cargo.toml` under `[build-dependencies]`, or simply call the CLI tool in a script or CI pipeline. It's good practice to have this as part of your build so that headers stay up-to-date.
Wait, I'm confused — should I write tests for my C bindings? Or is that separate?

Good question! While cbindgen handles the header generation, it doesn’t automatically test or validate your FFI code. You’ll still want to write integration tests in Rust and C (or C++) to ensure everything works as expected. This dual testing is crucial for avoiding subtle bugs.
Ah, that makes sense. Okay, so I should validate headers both manually by looking at them, but also have some kind of integration test? That’s a good plan?

Yes! You can use tools like `cargo cbindgen` in your CI pipeline to automatically generate the headers and then compile a small C program against it as part of your tests. This way, you get both documentation and validation.

topic: Build - cbindgen for Exporting Rust to C

turns.content: I'm trying to port this Java microservice to Rust, but I keep thinking about the old way of manually writing .h files and then compiling them. It feels like I'm back in the dark ages where you had to maintain a separate build script just for the C headers.

You're right, that manual approach is painful because every time you change a Rust function signature or add a doc comment, you have to update the header file and recompile everything. Think of it like having two separate maps for the same city; one for your app logic and another for the bridge design. cbindgen solves this by automatically generating that second map from your source code.
So instead of writing C headers manually, I just need to annotate my Rust functions? That sounds simpler, but how exactly do I tell it what to export?

Exactly. You mark your Rust functions with the `pub extern fn` syntax and then configure cbindgen in a config file called cbindgen.toml. This tool reads those annotations, understands the Rust type system, and generates a C header file for you.
What if I have some internal functions that shouldn't be exported to C? Do I still need to write them down somewhere?

You don't need to do anything special for the ones you don't want. cbindgen will automatically skip any function that isn't marked as `pub extern fn`. It only generates headers for symbols that are explicitly visible from C's perspective.
That makes sense, but what about the documentation? Will it show up in the generated header?

Yes. cbindgen propagates doc comments from your Rust source into the C header as `///` style documentation. You can configure this behavior in cbindgen.toml to decide exactly which functions get their docs included or excluded.
Okay, so I'm generating headers automatically. But how do I make sure the build pipeline actually uses these generated files?

You run cbindgen as part of your build process using Cargo's `cargo` command line tools. It outputs a file like out/cbindgen-header.h which you then compile with gcc or clang alongside your Rust binary.
What if I make a typo in the cbindgen.toml? Will it break my whole build?
It will fail, but that's actually good. The tool gives you clear error messages when your configuration is wrong or when a Rust function doesn't match what cbindgen expects.
Can I verify the generated headers are correct before they break my C code?
Yes. You can run cbindgen in verbose mode to see exactly what it's planning to generate, or you can compile a dummy C file against the header to catch any signature mismatches early.
I'm still worried about performance. Is there overhead when cbindgen runs?
There is some parsing overhead, but it's negligible compared to the time you'd spend manually maintaining headers and fixing signature mismatches later.
So my workflow becomes: write Rust code with cbindgen annotations, run cargo build which triggers cbindgen, then compile C against the generated header?
Precisely. That's a clean separation where your Rust compilation is independent from the C header generation, but they're tightly coupled through the configuration in cbindgen.toml.
One last thing: what happens if I change a Rust type that's used by an exported function? Does the header update automatically?
Yes. Since cbindgen parses your entire crate to understand types, any change in Rust types or function signatures will be reflected immediately in the next run of cbindgen.
